**Henry Howard, 7th Duke of Norfolk**, [KG](/wiki/Order_of_the_Garter "Order of the Garter") [PC](/wiki/Privy_Council_of_England "Privy Council of England") [FRS](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_Society "Fellow of the Royal Society") (11 January 1655 – 2 April 1701) was an English nobleman, politician, and soldier. He was the son of [Henry Howard, 6th Duke of Norfolk](/wiki/Henry_Howard,_6th_Duke_of_Norfolk "Henry Howard, 6th Duke of Norfolk"), and Lady Anne Somerset, daughter of [Edward Somerset, 2nd Marquess of Worcester](/wiki/Edward_Somerset,_2nd_Marquess_of_Worcester "Edward Somerset, 2nd Marquess of Worcester"), and Elizabeth Dormer. He was summoned to the House of Lords in his own right as Baron Mowbray in 1678. His unhappy marriage was the subject of much gossip, and ended in divorce.

## Marriage, separation and divorce

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Henry_Howard,_7th_Duke_of_Norfolk&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Marriage, separation and divorce")]

He married [Mary Mordaunt](/wiki/Mary_Howard,_Duchess_of_Norfolk_(d._1705) "Mary Howard, Duchess of Norfolk (d. 1705)"), the only surviving daughter and heiress of the [2nd Earl of Peterborough](/wiki/Henry_Mordaunt,_2nd_Earl_of_Peterborough "Henry Mordaunt, 2nd Earl of Peterborough") and Penelope O'Brien. They separated in 1685. He divorced her for her [adultery](/wiki/Adultery "Adultery") with Sir John Germain in 1700, after a previous attempt at [divorce](/wiki/Divorce "Divorce") in 1692 had failed when the House of Lords threw out his private Divorce Bill. Although he succeeded in obtaining damages in an action for [criminal conversation](/wiki/Criminal_conversation "Criminal conversation"), the details were so sordid that he may well have regretted the step, especially since the jury reduced his claim for £100,000 damages to £66, for which they were severely reprimanded by the presiding judge. The Duke was evidently in no position to condemn his wife's moral conduct: even his own counsel called it frankly a case of [the pot calling the kettle black](/wiki/The_pot_calling_the_kettle_black "The pot calling the kettle black").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

He died without children, and was succeeded by his nephew, [Thomas Howard, 8th Duke of Norfolk](/wiki/Thomas_Howard,_8th_Duke_of_Norfolk "Thomas Howard, 8th Duke of Norfolk"). His former wife married secondly [Sir John Germain, 1st Baronet](/wiki/Sir_John_Germain,_1st_Baronet "Sir John Germain, 1st Baronet"), who had been her lover for many years. She died in 1705.

He was rarely on good terms with his father, particularly after 1677 when his father married [Jane Bickerton](/wiki/Jane_Bickerton "Jane Bickerton"), his mistress of many years standing, an act which caused a violent family quarrel.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Later life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Henry_Howard,_7th_Duke_of_Norfolk&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Later life")]

Like almost all the Howards of Norfolk he was a devout [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic"), but during the anti-Catholic hysteria engendered by the [Popish Plot](/wiki/Popish_Plot "Popish Plot") he publicly conformed to the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England").[[3]](#cite_note-3) There is little doubt that this was simply a device to save the family estates. The ploy seems to have succeeded; although his father was charged with [recusancy](/wiki/Recusancy "Recusancy") in 1680, the charge was quickly dropped. While the senior Howard line survived unscathed, their cousin [William Howard, 1st Viscount Stafford](/wiki/William_Howard,_1st_Viscount_Stafford "William Howard, 1st Viscount Stafford"), was executed for his supposed part in the Plot in December 1680.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Henry as a peer in his own right, [Baron Mowbray](/wiki/Baron_Mowbray "Baron Mowbray"), sat as one of the peers who tried him. It is a sign of his moral courage and independent judgment, given the anti-Catholic feeling in the country, that he voted Not Guilty. This was the more notable since according to [John Evelyn](/wiki/John_Evelyn "John Evelyn"), of Stafford's extended family in the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"), (eight of them in all), he was the only one to do so, Stafford being a man "not beloved by his family".[[5]](#cite_note-5)

He became a [Fellow of the Royal Society](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_Society "Fellow of the Royal Society") in 1672. His father was already a fellow and was a generous benefactor of Society.

On 20 June 1685, he was appointed Colonel of the [Suffolk Regiment](/wiki/Suffolk_Regiment "Suffolk Regiment"), which at the time was called the Duke of Norfolk's Regiment of Foot. He was created a [Knight of the Garter](/wiki/Order_of_the_Garter "Order of the Garter") in the same year. As a man "all-powerful in his Dukedom"[[6]](#cite_note-6) he used his influence in the 1685 General Election to return members entirely loyal to the Crown (as his father had in 1673 when he found a safe seat at [Castle Rising](/wiki/Castle_Rising_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Castle Rising (UK Parliament constituency)") for [Samuel Pepys](/wiki/Samuel_Pepys "Samuel Pepys")). By 1688, however, he was on bad terms with [James II](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England"), openly disapproving of the King's aggressive policy of Catholic championship. When asked to question his constituents on whether they favoured repeal of the [Test Act](/wiki/Test_Act "Test Act"), he replied bluntly that he knew that all those in favour of repeal would fit comfortably in one coach.[[7]](#cite_note-7) When asked to replace the magistrates in his dukedom with more compliant ones he simply refused and prudently went to [France](/wiki/France "France"),[[8]](#cite_note-8) but returned in time to welcome the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution").

He served as a [Privy Councillor](/wiki/Privy_Council_of_England "Privy Council of England") under [William III](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") and [Mary II](/wiki/Mary_II_of_England "Mary II of England") in 1689. At first, he refused to take the oath necessary to sit in the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"), since although he had publicly conformed to the Anglican rite, it was no secret that he remained a Roman Catholic at heart; but after a few months, he subscribed to the oath. He continued to serve as [Lord Lieutenant](/wiki/Lord_Lieutenant "Lord Lieutenant") of Berkshire, Norfolk and Surrey, and *ex-officio* colonel of the [Berkshire Militia](/wiki/Berkshire_Militia "Berkshire Militia") under William and Mary.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The first [HMS *Norfolk*](/wiki/HMS_Norfolk_(1693) "HMS Norfolk (1693)") was named after him.

His private surgeon was [Thomas Greenhill](/wiki/Thomas_Greenhill_(surgeon) "Thomas Greenhill (surgeon)").

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Henry_Howard,_7th_Duke_of_Norfolk&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Death")]

A written [account](/wiki/Report "Report") of the death of the 7th Duke of Norfolk, who died in London on Wednesday ([**O.S.**](/wiki/Old_Style_and_New_Style_dates "Old Style and New Style dates")) 2 April 1701 by his secretary Francis Negus.

> Aprill ye 3rd 1701
>
> Sir,
>
> My Lord Duke died suddendly yesterday about eleven of the clock. His body was opened this night and all parts well, but destroyed with coagulated blood.
>
> Sir, Your afflicted humble servant.
>
> ffr. Negus
>
> For Mr Edward L'Estrange at Mileham Norfolke

— Norfolk lieutenancy journal, 1676-1701, transcribed and edited by Basil Cozens-Hardy, Norfolk Record Society, 1961.
